metadata.title: Original multi-difficulty CompTIA Security+ SY0-701 practice question bank
metadata.difficulty_profile: 100 questions each for easy, normal, hard, and extra-hard. Lower volume is intentional: the bank favors variety and question quality over repeating the same base scenario many times.
metadata.creation_method: Original questions generated from public objective alignment, official exam-format references, third-party training-pattern research, and community-reported study pain points. No practice-bank questions, exam dumps, or proprietary items were copied. Answer options are balanced so length, position, tone, and specificity are not reliable clues.
metadata.format_profile: Mixed single-best, first/next action, log-review, mini-PBQ-style, tradeoff, governance-evidence, and multi-response questions.
quality_controls: Each question maps to a Security+ SY0-701 objective and domain.
The four app-supported difficulties are used exactly: easy, normal, hard, extra-hard.
Domain distribution preserves the official 12/22/18/28/20 weighting per difficulty.
Correct answer length is checked against both longest-answer and shortest-answer bias.
Correct answer position distribution is checked to avoid A/B/C/D predictability.
Distractors are generated as plausible near misses in the same category as the correct answer.
BEST/FIRST/NEXT questions include a scenario constraint so only one answer best fits priority, risk, or business impact.
Mini-PBQ and log-review items use original artifacts and do not copy real exam or third-party question text.

questions.stem: A municipal government agency needs to stop unmanaged endpoints from reaching an internal finance application after several policy reminders failed. The team is under time pressure and must avoid treating a later governance step as the first technical move. The environment recently moved several workloads to cloud-hosted services. The organization is under a strict maintenance window and needs a rollback path. The priority is choosing the core concept without overengineering the response. Which action should the security team take FIRST?

choices.text: Publish a stronger acceptable use policy without technical enforcement, while leaving the root exposure unchanged.
Create a manual manager approval workflow for endpoint access outside the NAC enforcement path, but only after the issue becomes visible again.
Create a quarterly report showing which users connected from unmanaged devices.
Require device posture checks through NAC before allowing access to the finance network segment.

questions.explanation: NAC with posture checking is a technical preventive control that blocks noncompliant devices before access. Monitoring complements it, while policy-only or obscurity-based approaches do not enforce access. The distractors are near misses: they may improve process, monitoring, convenience, or documentation, but they do not best satisfy the priority in the scenario.
questions.copyright_note: Original practice item generated from objective alignment; not copied from a question bank or exam dump.

questions.stem: A manufacturing company with OT networks is presented with a simulated Security+ task: choose the control that best satisfies the scenario without relying on implicit trust or manual review alone. The task is tied to this condition: has an unsupported but mission-critical records system that cannot be patched for six months because the vendor is out of business. The environment recently moved several workloads to cloud-hosted services. The business owner wants the least disruptive control that still reduces material risk. The priority is choosing the core concept without overengineering the response. Which TWO responses BEST address the situation?

choices.text: Place the system in a tightly segmented network with allow-listed flows and monitoring, with monitoring, ownership, and rollback expectations defined.

Limit the legacy service to business hours while relying on users to report abnormal behavior, while leaving the root exposure unchanged, which can look reasonable in a policy review but does not address the operational failure.
Grant local administrator rights only to senior users, without proving that the risk is reduced.
Document the risk exception, owner approval, and expiration date for the compensating control.
Reduce logging on the legacy server to preserve performance during the exception period.

questions.explanation: When remediation is not immediately possible, segmentation, allow-listing, and monitoring reduce exposure as compensating controls. A formal exception keeps ownership and review visible. The distractors are near misses: they may improve process, monitoring, convenience, or documentation, but they do not best satisfy the priority in the scenario.
questions.copyright_note: Original practice item generated from objective alignment; not copied from a question bank or exam dump.

questions.stem: A financial services firm found that unauthorized firewall rule changes were made during overnight maintenance. Auditors, technical owners, and business owners all need evidence that the selected action matches the stated risk. A previous quick fix increased alert volume without reducing exposure. The control must work for remote users and branch locations. The team needs the most direct control for the stated risk. Which response creates the strongest defensible outcome?

choices.text: Relocate the firewall logs to cold storage immediately without changing the core control, but without enforcing the needed control.
Enable configuration change alerts and compare firewall state against an approved baseline.
Replace the firewall with a web application firewall.
Suspend broad administrator access until the next maintenance cycle without first validating the change path, but only after the issue becomes visible again.

questions.explanation: Detective controls identify unauthorized changes, and corrective controls restore the approved state. The other choices do not directly detect or correct the firewall configuration drift. The distractors are near misses: they may improve process, monitoring, convenience, or documentation, but they do not best satisfy the priority in the scenario.
questions.copyright_note: Original practice item generated from objective alignment; not copied from a question bank or exam dump.

questions.stem: A cloud-native startup is replacing broad VPN access after users on trusted subnets repeatedly reached systems outside their job duties. The team is under time pressure and must avoid treating a later governance step as the first technical move. The environment recently moved several workloads to cloud-hosted services. The change must be defensible during an external assessment. The priority is choosing the core concept without overengineering the response. Which action should the security team take FIRST?

choices.text: Use a single shared jump-box account for all privileged users.
Deploy policy enforcement points that evaluate identity, device posture, and context for each resource request.
Keep vpn access but rename the internal subnets to hide sensitive systems as the primary control path, but without enforcing the needed control.
Trust headquarters network location as the primary factor for privileged access decisions, but only after the issue becomes visible again.

questions.explanation: Zero Trust reduces implicit trust and makes access decisions based on identity, device state, policy, and context. Network location alone should not grant broad access. The distractors are near misses: they may improve process, monitoring, convenience, or documentation, but they do not best satisfy the priority in the scenario.
source_alignment: CompTIA Security+ SY0-701 1.2 Fundamental security concepts
questions.copyright_note: Original practice item generated from objective alignment; not copied from a question bank or exam dump.

questions.stem: A managed service provider is presented with a simulated Security+ task: match the cryptographic mechanism to the security property and key-management requirement. The task is tied to this condition: needs proof that a specific executive approved high-value wire transfers after several disputes about email-based authorization. A previous quick fix increased alert volume without reducing exposure. The environment contains regulated data and a hybrid mix of cloud and on-premises systems. The priority is choosing the core concept without overengineering the response. Which option BEST completes the simulated task?

choices.text: Use a VPN so approvals originate from internal IP space, while leaving the root exposure unchanged, which can look reasonable in a policy review but does not address the operational failure.
Encrypt all transfer emails with a shared team password, but without enforcing the needed control.
Hash the transfer amount but discard signer identity.
Use digital signatures tied to individual certificates for transfer approvals, then confirm that the original exposure or control gap is closed.

questions.explanation: Digital signatures provide integrity, authentication, and non-repudiation when tied to individual identities. Immutable logs strengthen evidence. Shared secrets and shared mailboxes weaken attribution. The distractors are near misses: they may improve process, monitoring, convenience, or documentation, but they do not best satisfy the priority in the scenario.
source_alignment: CompTIA Security+ SY0-701 1.2 Fundamental security concepts
questions.copyright_note: Original practice item generated from objective alignment; not copied from a question bank or exam dump.

questions.stem: A multinational manufacturer must investigate whether privileged database access followed an approved emergency process. Auditors, technical owners, and business owners all need evidence that the selected action matches the stated risk. The environment recently moved several workloads to cloud-hosted services. The organization is under a strict maintenance window and needs a rollback path. The team needs the most direct control for the stated risk. Which response creates the strongest defensible outcome?

choices.text: Review accounting logs that tie the administrator identity, time, command, and target database together.
Use a manual workflow where the administrator whether the access was appropriate.
Verify that the database server had disk encryption enabled, but without enforcing the needed control.
Rotate all user passwords before reviewing the access record, but without enforcing the needed control.

questions.explanation: Accounting records prove what authenticated and authorized users did. Correlating logs with tickets and recordings supports accountability for privileged actions. The distractors are near misses: they may improve process, monitoring, convenience, or documentation, but they do not best satisfy the priority in the scenario.
source_alignment: CompTIA Security+ SY0-701 1.2 Fundamental security concepts

questions.stem: A defense contractor sees outbound connections from a workstation to a file path that should never be opened by normal users. The team is under time pressure and must avoid treating a later governance step as the first technical move. The environment recently moved several workloads to cloud-hosted services. The business owner wants the least disruptive control that still reduces material risk. The priority is choosing the core concept without overengineering the response. Which action should the security team take FIRST?

choices.text: Investigate the workstation because access to a honeyfile is a high-confidence deception alert.
Replace endpoint protection with a perimeter firewall only.
Remove the honeyfile and rely on endpoint alerts before investigating the workstation, but only after the issue becomes visible again.
Treat the deception alert as proof of completed exfiltration and begin breach notification before scoping, without proving that the risk is reduced.

questions.explanation: Honeyfiles and honeytokens are designed to create high-signal alerts when touched. They indicate suspicious activity that warrants investigation, not automatic proof of full exfiltration. The distractors are near misses: they may improve process, monitoring, convenience, or documentation, but they do not best satisfy the priority in the scenario.
source_alignment: CompTIA Security+ SY0-701 1.2 Fundamental security concepts
questions.copyright_note: Original practice item generated from objective alignment; not copied from a question bank or exam dump.

questions.stem: A regional hospital is presented with a simulated Security+ task: choose the control that best satisfies the scenario without relying on implicit trust or manual review alone. The task is tied to this condition: plans to enforce a new deny-by-default egress rule set for production servers. A previous quick fix increased alert volume without reducing exposure. The control must work for remote users and branch locations. The priority is choosing the core concept without overengineering the response. Which TWO responses BEST address the situation?

choices.text: Document the change after implementation if no outage occurs, but without enforcing the needed control.
Apply the rule immediately because deny-by-default is always safer, but only after the issue becomes visible again, which may reduce friction for users but leaves the same attack path available.
Run the change first in a staging environment that mirrors production dependencies.
Approve the change only with tested allow-list rules, stakeholder signoff, a maintenance window, and a rollback plan, then validate the result with logs or testing.
Skip stakeholder review because firewall changes are purely technical.

questions.explanation: Security changes can create outages. Proper change management includes testing, ownership, impact analysis, maintenance windows, and a backout plan. The distractors are near misses: they may improve process, monitoring, convenience, or documentation, but they do not best satisfy the priority in the scenario.
questions.copyright_note: Original practice item generated from objective alignment; not copied from a question bank or exam dump.

questions.stem: A manufacturing company with OT networks wants to restart an authentication service during a certificate update but several legacy applications use undocumented dependencies. Auditors, technical owners, and business owners all need evidence that the selected action matches the stated risk. The environment recently moved several workloads to cloud-hosted services. The change must be defensible during an external assessment. The team needs the most direct control for the stated risk. Which response creates the strongest defensible outcome?

choices.text: Pause certificate validation in the legacy applications during the response window to reduce disruption, without proving that the risk is reduced.
Replace the certificate with a self-signed certificate and skip testing, but only after the issue becomes visible again.
Perform impact analysis and dependency mapping before scheduling the certificate change.
Relocate the change to friday evening without notifying stakeholders without changing the core control.

questions.explanation: Dependency mapping and impact analysis reduce the risk of security changes breaking legacy applications. Disabling validation or skipping communication creates avoidable risk. The distractors are near misses: they may improve process, monitoring, convenience, or documentation, but they do not best satisfy the priority in the scenario.
questions.copyright_note: Original practice item generated from objective alignment; not copied from a question bank or exam dump.

questions.stem: A financial services firm needs strong authentication between internal microservices after an attacker used a stolen API key from one service to query another. The team is under time pressure and must avoid treating a later governance step as the first technical move. A previous quick fix increased alert volume without reducing exposure. The environment contains regulated data and a hybrid mix of cloud and on-premises systems. The priority is choosing the core concept without overengineering the response. Which action should the security team take FIRST?

choices.text: Use mutual TLS with service-specific certificates issued by an internal CA.
Replace TLS with a checksum in each request header, while leaving the root exposure unchanged.
Place all services behind the same shared API key.
Encode the API key with Base64 before storing it, without proving that the risk is reduced.

questions.explanation: mTLS authenticates both parties and protects service-to-service communication. Managed key storage reduces key exposure. Shared keys and network trust alone are weaker. The distractors are near misses: they may improve process, monitoring, convenience, or documentation, but they do not best satisfy the priority in the scenario.
questions.copyright_note: Original practice item generated from objective alignment; not copied from a question bank or exam dump.

questions.stem: A cloud-native startup is presented with a simulated Security+ task: match the cryptographic mechanism to the security property and key-management requirement. The task is tied to this condition: must verify that downloaded deployment artifacts were not modified while still allowing anyone to validate integrity. The environment recently moved several workloads to cloud-hosted services. The organization is under a strict maintenance window and needs a rollback path. The priority is choosing the core concept without overengineering the response. Which option BEST completes the simulated task?

choices.text: Use reversible encoding so users can compare the encoded output, but without enforcing the needed control.
Publish a cryptographic hash and a digital signature for each artifact, and document the owner, approval, and verification evidence.
Compress the artifact to reduce the chance of tampering.
Encrypt the artifact with a shared password and email the password to all users, while leaving the root exposure unchanged.

questions.explanation: Hashes support integrity checks, while digital signatures bind the hash to a trusted signer. Encryption alone does not provide public integrity validation. The distractors are near misses: they may improve process, monitoring, convenience, or documentation, but they do not best satisfy the priority in the scenario.
questions.copyright_note: Original practice item generated from objective alignment; not copied from a question bank or exam dump.

questions.stem: A logistics company is deploying database field-level encryption for sensitive records and wants to reduce the chance that application administrators can extract keys. Auditors, technical owners, and business owners all need evidence that the selected action matches the stated risk. The environment recently moved several workloads to cloud-hosted services. The business owner wants the least disruptive control that still reduces material risk. The team needs the most direct control for the stated risk. Which response creates the strongest defensible outcome?

choices.text: Use a key management system with HSM-backed key protection and separation of duties.
Use one permanent key for all environments to simplify support.
Allow database administrators export keys for troubleshooting while monitoring for additional signs of impact, but without enforcing the needed control.
Hard-code keys in the application repository with restricted branch access, but only after the issue becomes visible again.

questions.explanation: A KMS backed by HSMs protects key material and supports rotation, audit, and separation of duties. Co-locating or hard-coding keys undermines encryption. The distractors are near misses: they may improve process, monitoring, convenience, or documentation, but they do not best satisfy the priority in the scenario.
questions.copyright_note: Original practice item generated from objective alignment; not copied from a question bank or exam dump.

questions.stem: A defense contractor observes low-and-slow credential harvesting, custom malware, and persistence targeting sensitive research over several months. The team is under time pressure and must avoid treating a later governance step as the first technical move. The team must distinguish between prevention, detection, response, and governance activities. The change must be defensible during an external assessment. The priority is choosing the core concept without overengineering the response. Which action should the security team take FIRST?

choices.text: Close the case after blocking one IP address, while leaving the root exposure unchanged.
Focus only on employee security awareness posters.
Treat it as if the attacker is only motivated by notoriety without further validation, but only after the issue becomes visible again.
Treat the activity as a likely advanced persistent threat and prioritize containment, threat hunting, and intelligence sharing.

questions.explanation: Long duration, custom tooling, persistence, and sensitive targeting align with APT behavior. Blocking a single indicator is not enough for a persistent adversary. The distractors are near misses: they may improve process, monitoring, convenience, or documentation, but they do not best satisfy the priority in the scenario.
source_alignment: CompTIA Security+ SY0-701 2.1 Threat actors and motivations

questions.stem: A regional hospital is presented with a simulated Security+ task: choose the control that best satisfies the scenario without relying on implicit trust or manual review alone. The task is tied to this condition: notices a departing employee bulk downloading design files outside normal hours after receiving a negative performance review. The security architect must balance confidentiality, integrity, and availability. The environment contains regulated data and a hybrid mix of cloud and on-premises systems. The priority is choosing the core concept without overengineering the response. Which option BEST completes the simulated task?

choices.text: Schedule follow-up until the employee leaves before reviewing access rather than changing the active control, but without enforcing the needed control.
Publish a company-wide memo naming the employee, but without enforcing the needed control, which may reduce friction for users but leaves the same attack path available.
Remove the employee account immediately without preserving logs after capturing a summary for later review.
Escalate as a potential insider threat and coordinate HR, legal, and security monitoring under policy, then validate the result with logs or testing.

questions.explanation: Insider threats often use valid access. Coordinated, policy-driven response preserves evidence and reduces unnecessary exposure. The distractors are near misses: they may improve process, monitoring, convenience, or documentation, but they do not best satisfy the priority in the scenario.
source_alignment: CompTIA Security+ SY0-701 2.1 Threat actors and motivations

questions.stem: A manufacturing company with OT networks discovers a business unit using an unsanctioned file-sharing service to collaborate with contractors on customer data. Auditors, technical owners, and business owners all need evidence that the selected action matches the stated risk. The team must distinguish between prevention, detection, response, and governance activities. The organization is under a strict maintenance window and needs a rollback path. The team needs the most direct control for the stated risk. Which response creates the strongest defensible outcome?

choices.text: Assess the shadow IT service, classify exposed data, and migrate collaboration to an approved controlled platform.
Allow the service because productivity increased temporarily under a documented exception, while leaving the root exposure unchanged.
Rely on users to delete files manually without verifying exposure through awareness guidance.
Block all cloud services without telling business units, without proving that the risk is reduced.

questions.explanation: Shadow IT creates governance, data handling, and monitoring gaps. The right response manages the data exposure and provides a sanctioned path. The distractors are near misses: they may improve process, monitoring, convenience, or documentation, but they do not best satisfy the priority in the scenario.
source_alignment: CompTIA Security+ SY0-701 2.1 Threat actors and motivations

questions.stem: A financial services firm receives a ransom demand that includes proof of stolen customer records and threats to publish the data. The team is under time pressure and must avoid treating a later governance step as the first technical move. The security architect must balance confidentiality, integrity, and availability. The security team needs a short-term containment step and a durable long-term fix. The priority is choosing the core concept without overengineering the response. Which action should the security team take FIRST?

choices.text: Treat it as if restoring from backup eliminates all reporting obligations without further validation, without proving that the risk is reduced.
Handle the event as financially motivated extortion involving both ransomware and data exfiltration risk.
Only reimage encrypted workstations and close the incident.
Classify the event as hacktivism because publication was threatened, without proving that the risk is reduced.

questions.explanation: Modern extortion often combines encryption with data theft. Restoration alone does not address disclosure, legal, or scope-of-compromise requirements. The distractors are near misses: they may improve process, monitoring, convenience, or documentation, but they do not best satisfy the priority in the scenario.
source_alignment: CompTIA Security+ SY0-701 2.1 Threat actors and motivations

questions.stem: A cloud-native startup is presented with a simulated Security+ task: place the preventive control at the right layer and verify that identity, logging, and deployment controls still apply. The task is tied to this condition: finds that several users granted a third-party OAuth application mailbox and file permissions after a fake productivity prompt. The team must distinguish between prevention, detection, response, and governance activities. The team has a small staff and needs a repeatable operational process. The priority is choosing the core concept without overengineering the response. Which TWO responses BEST address the situation?

choices.text: Train users to identify bad spelling as the only mitigation, but without enforcing the needed control.
Block the sender domain but leave granted application permissions active, without proving that the risk is reduced.
Revoke the malicious OAuth grants and restrict user consent to approved or verified applications, and document the owner, approval, and verification evidence.
Review mailbox rules and cloud audit logs for data access by the unauthorized application.
Force password resets only and assume tokens are invalidated.

questions.explanation: OAuth consent phishing can persist through granted tokens even after passwords change. Revoking grants and controlling app consent directly addresses the vector. The distractors are near misses: they may improve process, monitoring, convenience, or documentation, but they do not best satisfy the priority in the scenario.
source_alignment: CompTIA Security+ SY0-701 2.2 Threat vectors and attack surfaces
questions.copyright_note: Original practice item generated from objective alignment; not copied from a question bank or exam dump.

questions.stem: A logistics company identifies that a trusted software update package contains a malicious post-install script signed by a vendor account. Auditors, technical owners, and business owners all need evidence that the selected action matches the stated risk. The team must distinguish between prevention, detection, response, and governance activities. The change must be defensible during an external assessment. The team needs the most direct control for the stated risk. Which response creates the strongest defensible outcome?

choices.text: Replace endpoint detection with user awareness training, without proving that the risk is reduced.
Remove local logs to prevent script re-execution after capturing a summary for later review, but without enforcing the needed control.
Suspend deployment, validate package integrity through an out-of-band vendor channel, and hunt for affected hosts.
Block all vendor updates permanently.

questions.explanation: A signed package can still be malicious if the vendor pipeline or signing account is compromised. Verification, containment, and supply chain controls are required. The distractors are near misses: they may improve process, monitoring, convenience, or documentation, but they do not best satisfy the priority in the scenario.
source_alignment: CompTIA Security+ SY0-701 2.2 Threat vectors and attack surfaces

questions.stem: An energy utility detects employees connecting to a wireless network that mimics the corporate SSID but presents an untrusted certificate. The team is under time pressure and must avoid treating a later governance step as the first technical move. The security architect must balance confidentiality, integrity, and availability. The environment contains regulated data and a hybrid mix of cloud and on-premises systems. The priority is choosing the core concept without overengineering the response. Which action should the security team take FIRST?

choices.text: Use certificate-based enterprise Wi-Fi validation and educate users not to bypass certificate warnings.
Increase DHCP lease time on the real wireless network.
Switch the SSID to a more complex name, but without enforcing the needed control.
Temporarily allow users to bypass certificate warnings while the wireless issue is investigated, but only after the issue becomes visible again.

questions.explanation: Evil twin attacks exploit users connecting to fake access points. Enterprise authentication with certificate validation prevents credential capture. The distractors are near misses: they may improve process, monitoring, convenience, or documentation, but they do not best satisfy the priority in the scenario.
source_alignment: CompTIA Security+ SY0-701 2.2 Threat vectors and attack surfaces

questions.stem: A defense contractor is presented with a simulated Security+ task: choose the control that best satisfies the scenario without relying on implicit trust or manual review alone. The task is tied to this condition: reports that help desk staff reset MFA after callers spoof executive phone numbers and claim urgent travel lockout. The team must distinguish between prevention, detection, response, and governance activities. The organization is under a strict maintenance window and needs a rollback path. The priority is choosing the core concept without overengineering the response. Which option BEST completes the simulated task?

choices.text: Require identity-proofing procedures for high-risk help desk actions and block caller ID as a sole authenticator, while preserving the evidence needed to prove effectiveness.
Pause mfa for executives to reduce help desk calls during the response window to reduce disruption.
Train staff to trust calls from known phone numbers, but without enforcing the needed control.

Allow resets if the caller knows the employee title temporarily under a documented exception, but only after the issue becomes visible again, which creates documentation without changing the security outcome.

questions.explanation: Vishing and caller ID spoofing defeat weak help desk processes. Strong identity proofing and out-of-band verification reduce social engineering risk. The distractors are near misses: they may improve process, monitoring, convenience, or documentation, but they do not best satisfy the priority in the scenario.
source_alignment: CompTIA Security+ SY0-701 2.2 Threat vectors and attack surfaces
questions.copyright_note: Original practice item generated from objective alignment; not copied from a question bank or exam dump.

questions.stem: A regional hospital finds a workstation infected after a contractor inserted a promotional USB drive received at a conference. Auditors, technical owners, and business owners all need evidence that the selected action matches the stated risk. The security architect must balance confidentiality, integrity, and availability. The security team needs a short-term containment step and a durable long-term fix. The team needs the most direct control for the stated risk. Which response creates the strongest defensible outcome?

choices.text: Format all USB drives after use without scanning endpoints, but without enforcing the needed control.
Treat it as if encryption at rest prevents usb malware execution without further validation, but only after the issue becomes visible again.
Disable unauthorized removable media and use device control with malware scanning for approved exceptions.
Send user guidance that tells users to avoid unknown usb drives but allow unrestricted use.

questions.explanation: Removable media is a threat vector that needs technical device control, scanning, and safe alternatives. Awareness alone is not sufficient. The distractors are near misses: they may improve process, monitoring, convenience, or documentation, but they do not best satisfy the priority in the scenario.
source_alignment: CompTIA Security+ SY0-701 2.2 Threat vectors and attack surfaces

questions.stem: A manufacturing company with OT networks learns that users can change an account ID in an API request and retrieve another customer's invoice. The team is under time pressure and must avoid treating a later governance step as the first technical move. The team must distinguish between prevention, detection, response, and governance activities. The team has a small staff and needs a repeatable operational process. The priority is choosing the core concept without overengineering the response. Which action should the security team take FIRST?

choices.text: Increase TLS key length for API traffic, without proving that the risk is reduced.
Base64-encode invoice IDs in the URL, while leaving the root exposure unchanged.
Require users to clear browser cache.
Fix broken object-level authorization on the server side and test access checks for each object request.

questions.explanation: Object-level authorization must be enforced server side. Obfuscating IDs or hiding fields does not prevent direct API calls. The distractors are near misses: they may improve process, monitoring, convenience, or documentation, but they do not best satisfy the priority in the scenario.
questions.copyright_note: Original practice item generated from objective alignment; not copied from a question bank or exam dump.

questions.stem: A payment processor is presented with a simulated Security+ task: place the preventive control at the right layer and verify that identity, logging, and deployment controls still apply. The task is tied to this condition: finds production containers built from an image with critical vulnerabilities and packages not needed by the application. The team must distinguish between prevention, detection, response, and governance activities. The change must be defensible during an external assessment. The priority is choosing the core concept without overengineering the response. Which option BEST completes the simulated task?

choices.text: Limit exposure of the container runtime socket to the application with a time window instead of changing access control, while leaving the root exposure unchanged, which can look reasonable in a policy review but does not address the operational failure.
Patch the running container manually and skip rebuilding the image.
Rebuild from a minimal trusted base image, patch dependencies, and redeploy through the approved pipeline, with monitoring, ownership, and rollback expectations defined.
Run containers as root so patching is easier, but only after the issue becomes visible again.

questions.explanation: Containers should be rebuilt from patched, minimal images and deployed immutably. Image scanning and signing reduce supply chain and drift risk. The distractors are near misses: they may improve process, monitoring, convenience, or documentation, but they do not best satisfy the priority in the scenario.
questions.copyright_note: Original practice item generated from objective alignment; not copied from a question bank or exam dump.

questions.stem: A SaaS provider finds a storage bucket containing customer exports readable by anyone with the URL. Auditors, technical owners, and business owners all need evidence that the selected action matches the stated risk. The security architect must balance confidentiality, integrity, and availability. The environment contains regulated data and a hybrid mix of cloud and on-premises systems. The team needs the most direct control for the stated risk. Which response creates the strongest defensible outcome?

choices.text: Shorten generated URLs while leaving the bucket policy publicly readable.

questions.stem: A legal services company evaluates a control for an event with an asset value of 400000 USD, exposure factor of 25 percent, and annualized rate of occurrence of 0.2. Auditors, technical owners, and business owners all need evidence that the selected action matches the stated risk. Executives want evidence that the control addresses root cause rather than only the visible symptom. The business owner wants the least disruptive control that still reduces material risk. The team needs the most direct control for the stated risk. Which response creates the strongest defensible outcome?

choices.text: Calculate SLE as 100000 USD and ALE as 20000 USD before comparing control cost.
Use RTO as the annualized loss expectancy, while leaving the root exposure unchanged.
Defer action on aro because exposure factor already includes frequency until additional evidence is available, but without enforcing the needed control.
Calculate ALE as 400000 USD because asset value equals annual loss.

questions.explanation: SLE equals asset value times exposure factor, and ALE equals SLE times ARO. Here, 400000 x 0.25 = 100000; 100000 x 0.2 = 20000. The distractors are near misses: they may improve process, monitoring, convenience, or documentation, but they do not best satisfy the priority in the scenario.

questions.stem: A logistics company needs to stop unmanaged endpoints from reaching an internal finance application after several policy reminders failed. The implementation will become part of a standard operating procedure after validation. The solution must avoid creating a new single point of failure. A previous fix addressed symptoms but not the underlying control gap. Which response is the BEST choice?

choices.text: Create a quarterly report showing which users connected from unmanaged devices, and rely on manual oversight to catch failures after the fact.
Require device posture checks through NAC before allowing access to the finance network segment, while preserving the evidence needed to prove effectiveness.
Move the finance application to a less obvious DNS name while keeping access rules unchanged, which makes the option sound auditable but still leaves prevention or validation incomplete.
Create a manual manager approval workflow for endpoint access outside the NAC enforcement path, while deferring the technical control until a later review.

questions.explanation: NAC with posture checking is a technical preventive control that blocks noncompliant devices before access. questions.stem: An energy utility has an unsupported but mission-critical records system that cannot be patched for six months because the vendor is out of business. Analysts have only partial telemetry, so the decision has to rely on the strongest available indicator. The current architecture has legacy systems that cannot be immediately replaced. The team wants a response that can be repeated in similar incidents. Which response BEST fits the evidence?

choices.text: Grant local administrator rights only to senior users, and treat documentation as the primary mitigation.
Limit the legacy service to business hours while relying on users to report abnormal behavior.
Place the system in a tightly segmented network with allow-listed flows and enhanced monitoring.
Track the replacement project as the mitigation plan without adding interim exposure controls, while keeping the current access path or exposure mostly unchanged.

questions.explanation: When remediation is not immediately possible, segmentation, allow-listing, and monitoring reduce exposure as compensating controls. A formal exception keeps ownership and review visible. questions.stem: A defense contractor found that unauthorized firewall rule changes were made during overnight maintenance. Two stakeholders prefer a low-friction answer, but the security team must choose the option that changes the risk condition. The implementation will become part of a standard operating procedure after validation. The business owner wants the least disruptive control that still reduces material risk. A previous fix addressed symptoms but not the underlying control gap. Which TWO responses BEST address the situation?

choices.text: Increase password complexity for standard users only.
Use an approved rollback procedure to restore the last known good configuration after validation.
Relocate the firewall logs to cold storage immediately without changing the core control, and rely on manual oversight to catch failures after the fact.
Suspend broad administrator access until the next maintenance cycle without first validating the change path, while deferring the technical control until a later review.
Enable configuration change alerts and compare firewall state against an approved baseline.

questions.explanation: Detective controls identify unauthorized changes, and corrective controls restore the approved state. The other choices do not directly detect or correct the firewall configuration drift. The distractors are near misses: they may improve process, monitoring, convenience, or documentation, but they do not best satisfy the priority in the scenario.

questions.stem: A regional hospital is replacing broad VPN access after users on trusted subnets repeatedly reached systems outside their job duties. Analysts have only partial telemetry, so the decision has to rely on the strongest available indicator. The control must work for remote users and branch locations. A previous fix addressed symptoms but not the underlying control gap. Which response is the BEST choice?

choices.text: Trust headquarters network location as the primary factor for privileged access decisions, and rely on manual oversight to catch failures after the fact.

Trust corporate network location as the primary authorization signal, and treat documentation as the primary mitigation, which makes the option sound auditable but still leaves prevention or validation incomplete.
Deploy policy enforcement points that evaluate identity, device posture, and context for each resource request, with monitoring, ownership, and rollback expectations defined.
Keep vpn access but rename the internal subnets to hide sensitive systems as the primary control path.

questions.explanation: Zero Trust reduces implicit trust and makes access decisions based on identity, device state, policy, and context. Network location alone should not grant broad access. The distractors are near misses: they may improve process, monitoring, convenience, or documentation, but they do not best satisfy the priority in the scenario.

questions.stem: A telehealth platform needs proof that a specific executive approved high-value wire transfers after several disputes about email-based authorization. The analyst also sees this evidence: `siem: correlated_events=3 sources=endpoint,identity,network severity=high confidence=medium`. Analysts have only partial telemetry, so the decision has to rely on the strongest available indicator. The recommendation must minimize downtime and preserve auditability. The team wants a response that can be repeated in similar incidents. Which response BEST fits the evidence?

choices.text: Allow assistants to approve transfers from a shared mailbox temporarily under a documented exception.
Hash the transfer amount but discard signer identity, and rely on manual oversight to catch failures after the fact.
Use digital signatures tied to individual certificates for transfer approvals.
Encrypt all transfer emails with a shared team password, while keeping the current access path or exposure mostly unchanged.

questions.explanation: Digital signatures provide integrity, authentication, and non-repudiation when tied to individual identities. Immutable logs strengthen evidence. Shared secrets and shared mailboxes weaken attribution. The distractors are near misses: they may improve process, monitoring, convenience, or documentation, but they do not best satisfy the priority in the scenario.
source_alignment: CompTIA Security+ SY0-701 1.2 Fundamental security concepts

questions.stem: A payment processor must investigate whether privileged database access followed an approved emergency process. Two stakeholders prefer a low-friction answer, but the security team must choose the option that changes the risk condition. The implementation will become part of a standard operating procedure after validation. The solution must avoid creating a new single point of failure. A previous fix addressed symptoms but not the underlying control gap. Which option most directly addresses the root cause without overcorrecting?

choices.text: Review centralized accounting logs that tie the administrator identity, time, command, and target database together.
Use a manual workflow where the administrator whether the access was appropriate.
Check only whether the password met complexity requirements, and rely on manual oversight to catch failures after the fact.
Verify that the database server had disk encryption enabled, and treat documentation as the primary mitigation.

questions.explanation: Accounting records prove what authenticated and authorized users did. Correlating logs with tickets and recordings supports accountability for privileged actions. The distractors are near misses: they may improve process, monitoring, convenience, or documentation, but they do not best satisfy the priority in the scenario.

questions.stem: A SaaS provider sees outbound connections from a workstation to a file path that should never be opened by normal users. Analysts have only partial telemetry, so the decision has to rely on the strongest available indicator. The current architecture has legacy systems that cannot be immediately replaced. A previous fix addressed symptoms but not the underlying control gap. Which response is the BEST choice?

choices.text: Replace endpoint protection with a perimeter firewall only.
Exclude the workstation from deception alerts because the honeyfile was intentionally planted, while deferring the technical control until a later review.
Investigate the workstation because access to a honeyfile is a high-confidence deception alert, then confirm that the original exposure or control gap is closed.
Treat the deception alert as proof of completed exfiltration and begin breach notification before scoping, while keeping the current access path or exposure mostly unchanged.

questions.explanation: Honeyfiles and honeytokens are designed to create high-signal alerts when touched. They indicate suspicious activity that warrants investigation, not automatic proof of full exfiltration. The distractors are near misses: they may improve process, monitoring, convenience, or documentation, but they do not best satisfy the priority in the scenario.

questions.stem: A logistics company plans to enforce a new deny-by-default egress rule set for production servers. The analyst also sees this evidence: `siem: correlated_events=3 sources=endpoint,identity,network severity=high confidence=medium`. The implementation will become part of a standard operating procedure after validation. The business owner wants the least disruptive control that still reduces material risk. The team wants a response that can be repeated in similar incidents. Which TWO responses BEST address the situation?

choices.text: Approve the change only with tested allow-list rules, stakeholder signoff, a maintenance window, and a rollback plan.
Document the change after implementation if no outage occurs, while deferring the technical control until a later review.
Apply the rule immediately because deny-by-default is always safer, while deferring the technical control until a later review.
Run the change first in a staging environment that mirrors production dependencies, while preserving the evidence needed to prove effectiveness.

Pause all outbound traffic permanently and wait for service owners to complain during the response window to reduce disruption, which reduces short-term disruption but preserves the condition that enabled the finding.

questions.explanation: Security changes can create outages. Proper change management includes testing, ownership, impact analysis, maintenance windows, and a backout plan. The distractors are near misses: they may improve process, monitoring, convenience, or documentation, but they do not best satisfy the priority in the scenario.

questions.stem: An energy utility wants to restart an authentication service during a certificate update but several legacy applications use undocumented dependencies. Analysts have only partial telemetry, so the decision has to rely on the strongest available indicator. The control must work for remote users and branch locations. choices.text: Pause certificate validation in the legacy applications during the response window to reduce disruption.
Relocate the change to friday evening without notifying stakeholders without changing the core control, and treat documentation as the primary mitigation.
Perform impact analysis and dependency mapping before scheduling the certificate change.
Restart the service immediately because certificate updates improve security, and treat documentation as the primary mitigation.

questions.explanation: Dependency mapping and impact analysis reduce the risk of security changes breaking legacy applications. Disabling validation or skipping communication creates avoidable risk. questions.stem: A retail chain needs strong authentication between internal microservices after an attacker used a stolen API key from one service to query another. Analysts have only partial telemetry, so the decision has to rely on the strongest available indicator. The recommendation must minimize downtime and preserve auditability. A previous fix addressed symptoms but not the underlying control gap. Which response is the BEST choice?

choices.text: Allow any service in the subnet to call any other service temporarily under a documented exception, while keeping the current access path or exposure mostly unchanged.
Use mutual TLS with service-specific certificates issued by an internal CA, then validate the result with logs or testing.
Encode the API key with Base64 before storing it.
Replace TLS with a checksum in each request header, and treat documentation as the primary mitigation.

questions.explanation: mTLS authenticates both parties and protects service-to-service communication. Managed key storage reduces key exposure. Shared keys and network trust alone are weaker. questions.stem: A legal services company must verify that downloaded deployment artifacts were not modified while still allowing anyone to validate integrity. The analyst also sees this evidence: `siem: correlated_events=3 sources=endpoint,identity,network severity=high confidence=medium`. The implementation will become part of a standard operating procedure after validation. The solution must avoid creating a new single point of failure. The team wants a response that can be repeated in similar incidents. Which response BEST fits the evidence?

choices.text: Publish a cryptographic hash and a digital signature for each artifact.
Store the artifact in a hidden directory without validation metadata, while deferring the technical control until a later review.
Use reversible encoding so users can compare the encoded output, while keeping the current access path or exposure mostly unchanged.
Compress the artifact to reduce the chance of tampering.

questions.explanation: Hashes support integrity checks, while digital signatures bind the hash to a trusted signer. Encryption alone does not provide public integrity validation. questions.stem: A telehealth platform is deploying database field-level encryption for sensitive records and wants to reduce the chance that application administrators can extract keys. Two stakeholders prefer a low-friction answer, but the security team must choose the option that changes the risk condition. Analysts have only partial telemetry, so the decision has to rely on the strongest available indicator. The current architecture has legacy systems that cannot be immediately replaced. A previous fix addressed symptoms but not the underlying control gap. Which option most directly addresses the root cause without overcorrecting?

choices.text: Store encryption keys in the same database table as the encrypted data, while deferring the technical control until a later review.
Use a centralized key management system with HSM-backed key protection and separation of duties.
Use one permanent key for all environments to simplify support, and rely on manual oversight to catch failures after the fact.
Hard-code keys in the application repository with restricted branch access.

questions.explanation: A KMS backed by HSMs protects key material and supports rotation, audit, and separation of duties. Co-locating or hard-coding keys undermines encryption. questions.stem: A SaaS provider observes low-and-slow credential harvesting, custom malware, and persistence targeting sensitive research over several months. A previous quick fix increased alert volume without reducing exposure. The control must work for remote users and branch locations. A previous fix addressed symptoms but not the underlying control gap. Which response is the BEST choice?

choices.text: Classify the activity as an unskilled attacker because there is no immediate ransom note.
Close the case after blocking one IP address, and treat documentation as the primary mitigation.
Treat the activity as a likely advanced persistent threat and prioritize containment, threat hunting, and intelligence sharing, then confirm that the original exposure or control gap is closed.

Treat it as if the attacker is only motivated by notoriety without further validation, while keeping the current access path or exposure mostly unchanged, which reduces short-term disruption but preserves the condition that enabled the finding.

questions.explanation: Long duration, custom tooling, persistence, and sensitive targeting align with APT behavior. Blocking a single indicator is not enough for a persistent adversary. Schedule follow-up until the employee leaves before reviewing access rather than changing the active control, while deferring the technical control until a later review.
Remove the employee account immediately without preserving logs after capturing a summary for later review, while deferring the technical control until a later review.

questions.explanation: Insider threats often use valid access. Coordinated, policy-driven response preserves evidence and reduces unnecessary exposure. choices.text: Classify the event as hacktivism because publication was threatened, and treat documentation as the primary mitigation.
Handle the event as financially motivated extortion involving both ransomware and data exfiltration risk, then validate the result with logs or testing.
Pay immediately before validating the scope of compromise.

questions.stem: A legal services company finds that several users granted a third-party OAuth application mailbox and file permissions after a fake productivity prompt. The analyst also sees this evidence: `idp_audit: grant_type=consent app=unverified scope=mail.read files.read result=success`. The environment recently moved several workloads to cloud-hosted services. The business owner wants the least disruptive control that still reduces material risk. The team wants a response that can be repeated in similar incidents. Which TWO responses BEST address the situation?

choices.text: Block the sender domain but leave granted application permissions active, and treat documentation as the primary mitigation, which is administratively convenient but weaker than addressing the control gap directly.
Review mailbox rules and cloud audit logs for data access by the unauthorized application, with monitoring, ownership, and rollback expectations defined.
Train users to identify bad spelling as the only mitigation.
Block all email access during the investigation without first scoping affected mailboxes, while deferring the technical control until a later review.
Revoke the malicious OAuth grants and restrict user consent to approved or verified applications.

questions.explanation: OAuth consent phishing can persist through granted tokens even after passwords change. Revoking grants and controlling app consent directly addresses the vector. The distractors are near misses: they may improve process, monitoring, convenience, or documentation, but they do not best satisfy the priority in the scenario.

questions.stem: A telehealth platform identifies that a trusted software update package contains a malicious post-install script signed by a vendor account. choices.text: Remove local logs to prevent script re-execution after capturing a summary for later review, and rely on manual oversight to catch failures after the fact.

Require users to clear browser cache, which makes the option sound auditable but still leaves prevention or validation incomplete, which reduces short-term disruption but preserves the condition that enabled the finding.

Transfer the risk by buying insurance while keeping the service exposed, and add compensating monitoring that would detect recurrence but not prevent the scenario from repeating.

questions.explanation: If a risky activity is not needed, retiring it avoids the risk. The decision should be documented and technically verified. The distractors are near misses: they may improve process, monitoring, convenience, or documentation, but they do not best satisfy the priority in the scenario.
